reasoning_summary: The fresh refactor baseline run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06 measured 24.408 ms, which is still about 1.87 ms slower than the local cuBLASLt reference at roughly 22.29 ms. The custom kernel already carries 16.62 active warps, so the dominant gap is not raw occupancy versus the vendor path; it is the much larger synchronization and latency cost inside the current hot-band 128x128 PTX microkernel. The custom profile shows 5.49% barrier stalls and 7.19% long-scoreboard stalls, while the cuBLAS reference stays around 0.16% barrier and 0.19% long-scoreboard on the same workload. That makes the best next move a low-risk launch swap to the already-implemented 128x128x32 staged hot-band kernel, followed by a deeper rewrite that collapses the current PTX microkernel wait/sync cadence, with the 64x384 row-band split rebalance held as the third lever because the old autotune sweep already identified 384-wide hot-band tiling as the strongest measured shape family.

reasoning_summary: Round 1 already falsified the simplest barrier-reduction hypothesis: swapping the default hot-band launch to the existing 128x128x32 staged sibling pushed runtime from 24.408 ms to 30.008 ms, increased barrier stalls from 5.49% to 10.09%, and raised registers per thread from 200 to 212. That means the next round should not keep exploring deeper K staging on top of the regressed head. The accepted base remains the 24.408 ms PTX hot-band launch, and the better next move is to reduce how much work flows through that hotspot rather than making its stages even heavier. The strongest measured family evidence still points at the 64x384 hot-band path from the fixed-tile sweep, so the top-ranked direction is to restore the accepted base and expand the peeled 64x384 row-band share. The second direction is a smaller launch-order change inside the accepted PTX path by retuning kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows. The third direction keeps the same 128x128 single-K staging depth but swaps away from the microkernel-specific accumulate path to the existing non-microkernel sibling. The cuBLAS reference remains useful as a ceiling: it tolerates similar register pressure yet keeps barrier stalls near zero, so the custom search should focus on reducing barrier exposure and wasted hot-band scheduling work, not on adding more shared-memory staging.
notes: Round 2 should branch from the accepted base run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06, not from the round-1 regressed staged-kernel head. The top priority is to reduce time spent in the 128x128 hotspot rather than add more K-stage depth there.

reasoning_summary: Round 2 falsified the row-band expansion family much more strongly than the raw PTX kernel profile alone suggests. The profiled 128x128 PTX hotspot itself improved in almost every local metric versus the round-1 staged regression: runtime-facing tensor activity rose to 48.45%, barrier stalls dropped to 5.98%, registers fell to 198, and compute/memory throughput both improved. Yet total end-to-end runtime exploded to 47.291 ms. That means the extra 64x384 peeled work is far more expensive than the NCU-selected hotspot implies, so the search should stop expanding that family and return to the accepted base. The next best move is therefore a cheap, isolated launch-order tweak on top of the accepted PTX hot-band path: retune kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows to change how many row tiles share the same hot-band B tile traversal. The second-best move keeps the same row split but swaps to the existing single-K 128x128 non-microkernel sibling, isolating whether the microkernel-specific accumulate ordering is hurting scoreboard behavior. The third move is a more speculative launch-bounds retune to see whether the compiler can trim register pressure further without spills, but it ranks lower because the cuBLAS reference already shows that lower active-warps alone is not the main gap.
notes: Round 3 should return to the accepted base run 20260421_133418_bf16_gemm_v1_c859cd06 before implementing any new direction. The 64x384 row-band expansion family is now explicitly deprioritized after the 47.291 ms regression.

reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_152228_bf16_gemm_v1_2fbb368d at 24.392608 ms after swapping the default hot-band launch to the single-K 128x128 regular sibling. That edit produced a real but incomplete recovery: runtime improved by 0.144 ms versus the previous grouped-row regression, registers dropped from 200 to 196, and long-scoreboard stall improved from 7.33% to 7.15%. But the run is still 0.069087 ms slower than the clean accepted base 20260421_150626_bf16_gemm_v1_6cc462c4 at 24.323521 ms, and it remains 2.392608 ms behind the clean cuBLAS reference. So the kernel-variant swap moved the machine state in the right direction without solving the underlying latency-hiding wall. Because the user explicitly asked to widen family coverage from master heuristic history, the frontier was reseeded with several materially different families. The best next round is the lowest-risk exploit from that widened set: hoist invariant shared-offset math out of the active 128x128 steady-state loops so the current regular sibling keeps its small register win while shaving loop-carried control overhead. The second choice is a more aggressive PTX barrier-surgery family that returns to the accepted PTX surface without reintroducing the two-K-stage shared-memory blowup. The third choice is the high-ceiling 256x128 half-panel register-budget transplant, which stays queued because it is still the strongest way to attack the 16.6%-warps plateau, but it is too correctness-sensitive to auto-select ahead of the cheaper 128x128 exploit. The new persistent human guidance is now part of the search context as well. It explicitly biases the frontier toward 256x128 / 64x64 tiling, wide global-memory access, shared-memory reuse, async-copy pipelines, bank-conflict avoidance, L2 swizzle, register reuse, and deeper Pg2s/Ps2r/stage buffering. For this round that guidance is treated as a strong argument to keep the 256x128 transplant family and the PTX pipeline/barrier families hot in the widened frontier, even though the immediate recommendation still stays with the lower-risk 128x128 offset-hoist exploit on the current live surface.
notes: Round 3 explicitly folds master heuristic-history families into the ranking. The just-measured non-microkernel sibling is treated as partial positive signal, not a new base: it improved the previous regressed run but still did not reclaim the clean accepted runtime. Persistent human guidance from state/human_guidance.md is now part of every frontier-search read set.

reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_153021_bf16_gemm_v1_24f31aab at 24.195072 ms after the steady-state shared-offset hoist. That was a real improvement of 0.198 ms over the prior regular-sibling run and it became the new clean accepted base. But the machine state barely moved: registers stayed at 196, active warps stayed at 16.61%, long-scoreboard slightly worsened from 7.15% to 7.21%, and barrier remained flat at 5.49%. So the offset-hoist was useful cleanup, not a structural answer to the remaining 2.195 ms cuBLAS gap. At this point the active 128x128 path looks locally polished enough that the next round should follow the new persistent human guidance more directly. That guidance explicitly prefers 256x128 block tiling, 64x64 warp tiling, wider/coalesced access, stronger shared-memory reuse, async copy overlap, bank-conflict avoidance, L2-aware swizzle, register reuse, and deeper Pg2s/Ps2r/stage buffering. The cleanest immediate embodiment of that guidance is the existing in-tree 256x128 pivot hot-band kernel, which is a bounded geometry change rather than a correctness-sensitive rewrite. The second choice is the higher-ceiling half-panel register-budget transplant into that same 256x128 pivot family. The third choice keeps the PTX barrier-trim family hot as an orthogonal fallback, because the accepted 128x128/PTX surface still remains the most stable reference if the 256x128 geometry family disappoints.
notes: Round 4 explicitly lets the persistent human guidance pull the ranking toward 256x128 families. The round-3 hoist win is accepted as a useful 128x128 cleanup, but it did not move occupancy or scoreboard enough to justify another small 128x128-only exploit first.

reasoning_summary: The live run context is 20260421_153357_bf16_gemm_v1_f1c576ee at 30.173615 ms after promoting the existing 256x128 pivot hot-band kernel. That experiment is a clean correctness pass but a decisive performance failure: runtime regressed by 5.979 ms, tensor activity collapsed from 48.33% to 36.77%, shared memory jumped from 22,016 B to 42,496 B, and barrier stall rose from 5.49% to 8.32%. At the same time, the run also showed why the 256x128 family is still not dead: registers fell to 167 and long-scoreboard collapsed to 2.19%, which means the geometry does attack part of the machine-state problem, just not in a shippable form yet. From round 5 onward the human guidance has to be reviewed explicitly, and the latest evidence now maps it item by item. Tiling 256x128 / 64x64 is accepted as a promising axis, but simple promotion is now rejected because it explodes shared footprint and barrier cost. Coalescing access and async copy are already present, so they are guardrails rather than the missing idea. Data reuse, Pg2s, Ps2r, stage design, bank-conflict control, and especially register reuse remain accepted because the failed pivot suggests the 256x128 family only works if those details are improved together rather than by geometry alone. L2-cache swizzle stays relevant because the pivot run also dropped L2 throughput materially. That makes the best next move the correctness-safe 256x128 pivot plus half-panel register-budget transplant, because it is the most direct way to salvage the human-guided 256x128 branch without repeating the already-failed simple promotion. The second choice is to stay on the current accepted non-PTX 128x128 sibling and force 3-CTA residency, which is a bounded occupancy experiment on the current best clean base. The third choice keeps the PTX barrier-trim family alive as an orthogonal fallback on the small-shared-footprint surface.
notes: Round 5 human-guidance review: Tiling(accept but reject simple 256x128 promotion), Coalescing Access(guardrail, already present), Data Reuse(accept), Async Copy(guardrail, already present), Bank Conflict(accept), L2 Cache(accept as secondary), Register Reuse(strong accept), Pg2s(accept), Ps2r(accept), Stage(accept but reject shared-memory blowup). The simple 256x128 pivot is now treated as a measured negative; only deeper 256x128 salvage or bounded occupancy probes stay active.
